data.title: Signs Of Life For Bitcoin
data.description:
It was a rough six months for Bitcoin (BTC-USD) longs, as the price of BTC fell by more than half from an all-time high of $126,251 on October 6th, down to the low $60,000s in Q1.

Things have turned more positive this month, though, as Bitcoin has risen from $68k at the end of March up to $79k today.

As shown below, the rally over the last week or two has pushed Bitcoin's price above the top of the downtrend channel that had formed off the highs made late last year. We've also seen a series of higher lows since the intraday low was made on February 24th.

If Bitcoin continues higher, its next test will be right around $85k, which acted as support from last November through January before ultimately giving way.

That prior support will now act as resistance that could be difficult to break through on a first or second test.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Nvidia: Trading At A Rare Discount Just As Its Next AI Supercycle Kicks Off
data.description:
Performance Assessment

Nvidia Corporation (NVDA) has started to break out of its flat performance rut, resuming its outperformance vs. the broader market index:

Elevator Pitch

I am once again getting more bullish on Nvidia due to some encouraging fresh data:

  1. A severe shortage in GPUs implies pricing tailwinds and sustained demand visibility.
  2. A strong March uptick in a key supplier's revenues bodes well for NVDA's outlook.
  3. Nvidia is facing more intense competition in China.
  4. Mr. Market is giving us an opportunity to load up.
  5. NVDA stock is rallying hard from a key support level.

A Severe Shortage In GPUs Implies Pricing Tailwinds And Sustained Demand Visibility

Nvidia's GPU availability has collapsed to the lowest levels since late 2023 to early 2024:

I believe this gives Nvidia plenty of pricing leverage and sustained demand visibility for them to have the confidence to invest aggressively in newer and better products. In an earlier article on NVDA, I had noted how the company committed to shortening its product release cycle to 1 year, down from 2 years. And the next section gives us clues that execution on this is probably going well:

A Strong March Uptick In A Key Supplier's Revenues Bodes Well For NVDA's Outlook

Foxconn (FXCOF) is a key supplier to Nvidia as it sells more than 50% of Nvidia's AI hardware. So I keep track of this company's monthly revenues to get clues on Nvidia's production outlook. And the latest March '26 revenue print is very encouraging:

As you can see in the chart above, Foxconn used to have a dip in the Jan to Mar period (Feb is not as reliable as it is nerfed by fewer days in the month and the Chinese New Year holidays). But over the years, this dip has gotten smaller and smaller, and this year, the Mar'26 quarter actually saw a sharp uptick that put its monthly sales figure 10% higher than that of Jan'26.

In Foxconn's commentary, they attributed the Mar'26 sales strength to:

strong pull-in momentum from new product launches.

- Foxconn's Mar'26 revenue report.

I suspect that this may be related to the production ramp-up in the next-generation Vera Rubin platform, which is expected to launch in the second half of this year. Overall, I view this as a positive signal that Nvidia's next major product launch may be on track. This is still an important piece of information since history has shown us that delays in new product launches can cause the stock to correct, even if sideways.

Nvidia Is Facing More Intense Competition In China

One key risk factor to monitor is Nvidia's market position in China. It looks like the U.S. semiconductor export controls have motivated China to become more self-reliant, prompting them to invest in their own chip ecosystems. And now, local Chinese companies such as Huawei and Alibaba (BABA) are gaining strong traction and market share (41% share in the AI accelerator market in 2025).

Now, Nvidia is still the market leader in China with a 55% share. But the point is that local Chinese competition is gradually chipping away at Nvidia's market-leading position. And with the government fostering local players, I expect Nvidia's position in China to weaken further. This can be a key headwind for the overall company, as China (including Hong Kong) makes up 12.3% of total revenues:

Mr. Market Is Giving Us An Opportunity To Load Up

NVDA stock's 1-yr fwd P/E is at 24.0x today, which represents a 23.3% discount to its semiconductor comps' median multiple of 31.3x:

I am a bit surprised that NVDA continues to trade at trough relative valuations. A 23% discount is rather unusual for NVDA, as it has usually commanded a 1-yr fwd P/E closer to its comps' median levels:

NVDA's valuation multiple is close to that of the overall S&P 500 Index (SP500), which currently has a P/E of 21.6x. This is despite Nvidia being expected to contribute a whopping 21% of the S&P 500's overall earnings growth in FY26.

The bottom line is that I think Mr. Market is giving us a great opportunity to load up on NVDA stock.

NVDA Stock Is Rallying Hard From A Key Support Level

If this is your first time reading a Hunting Alpha article using Technical Analysis, you may want to read this post, which explains how and why I read the charts the way I do. All my charts reflect total shareholder return, as they are adjusted for dividends/distributions.

NVDA Technical Analysis

NVDA stock has printed a convincing bullish push from the key weekly support level I had highlighted in my last article. I think there are clear skies ahead for the uptrend to resume.

Takeaway & Positioning

  • Fundamental Thesis: Bullish as a GPU shortage environment and readings from suppliers' monthly revenues spell a solid pricing and demand outlook for Nvidia, even as it faces more competition in China.
  • Valuation: Bullish, as NVDA seems surprisingly undervalued with a 1-yr fwd P/E at a major discount vs. its broader semiconductor peers and close to the overall S&P 500 index.
  • Technical Analysis: Bullish as the stock is rallying hard after bouncing from a support level.
  • Final Assessment and Rating: Bullish, Strong Buy.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Pony AI: A Big Year Ahead As Company Guides To Tripling Robotaxi Revenue
data.description:
Though the S&P 500 has rallied back near all-time highs amid investor hopes for a resolution of the conflict in Iran, many small and mid-cap stocks remain well off recent highs. Burned by the notion of a "SaaSpocalypse," it appears that the market has taken more of a cautious approach toward investing in cutting-edge new technology. This isn't just limited to AI but other early-revenue trends like autonomous vehicles as well.

Against this backdrop, Pony AI (PONY), an autonomous vehicle software developer that is dual-headquartered between China and Silicon Valley, has seen its share price erode ~30% this year. Though the stock still remains sharply up over a 1-year timeframe, I think the gains are more than justified alongside Pony's tremendous R&D milestones, and the recent dip gives us an excellent opportunity to buy this stock well off 2025 highs.

I last wrote a "Buy" article on Pony in January, when the stock was trading at $17 per share. While I am frustrated with the stock's ~33% decline since then, its losses are in line with similar small/mid-cap tech peers, especially decliners in the software sector. Meanwhile, given the strength of the company's recently released Q4 earnings print, I see very little reason to be concerned about Pony's fundamental trajectory. I'm reiterating my "Buy" rating here.

As a reminder for investors who are newer to Pony, here is what I consider to be the core drivers of the bull case for this name:

  • Multiple revenue streams for its technology. Pony is one of the few technology companies in the world to develop L-4, or fully autonomous vehicle driving software without human assistance. The company's principal vehicle for monetization is through its consumer-facing robotaxi services, but it also has enterprise delivery revenue from its robotaxi fleet while also licensing its software to third-party OEMs. The full suite of revenue diversification gives Pony plenty of opportunities to monetize its R&D.
  • Still nascent operating areas for robotaxi services. Currently, Pony's robotaxi services only serve a few Chinese cities: Beijing, Guangzhou, Shenzhen, and Shanghai. Major routes in Beijing and Shenzhen were recently added. The company is pushing deeper into "Tier 2" cities in China, like Hangzhou and Changsha, to fuel its next phase of growth, while also eyeing a launch in the San Francisco Bay Area.
  • Breakeven unit economics in mature markets. The company noted that in February 2026, one of its densest markets, Shenzhen, achieved unit economics breakeven, proving that at scale, robotaxis and AVs are a highly viable business model.
  • Top-tier partner network. Pony isn't limited just to its own platform to scale. The company has struck partnerships with Uber (UBER) and Bolt to expand its presence overseas and leverage their deep customer networks.

Stay long here and use this year's dip as a buying opportunity.

Q4 download

Let's now go through Pony's latest quarterly results in greater detail. The Q4 earnings summary is shown below:

Pony's revenue in Q4 declined 18% y/y to $29.1 million. However, we note that the decline was primarily driven by a tough y/y comp in licensing revenue, which has very lumpy timing (in Q3, Pony recognized $8.6 million in licensing revenue, which grew at greater than a 3x y/y pace).

The core business that we're most interested in is the growth in Pony's robotaxi services, which, as a reminder, began FY25 in a very nascent state. In Q4, robotaxi revenue grew 160% y/y to $6.7 million.

As a reminder, the robotaxi division itself generates revenue not just from core fare-charging revenue (which is generated by the company's own fleet of robotaxis) but also from technology licensing to third-party platforms and project-based services. In Q4, while the company no longer provided the exact breakout of fare-charging revenue, we note that fares rose at greater than a 500% y/y pace, accelerating sharply versus 233% y/y growth in fare-charging revenue in Q3.

The chart above, meanwhile, showcases more detailed metrics in the robotaxi business. We note that in Q4, Pony's fleet averaged 23 daily orders, with each car generating RMB 338 (roughly $50 in U.S. dollar terms) in daily revenue.

This is important because, in my view, there is plenty of opportunity to increase these averages. Robotaxis don't have drivers who need to rest, and they can run 24 hours a day if needed. The fact that each car is utilized only 23 times per day suggests that the average trip time plus idle time is more than 1 hour per passenger. Especially as Pony is focused on providing services in dense urban cores (where trips are generally quicker and last-mile, perhaps only 10-15 minutes between rides), there is substantial opportunity for Pony to increase utilization of its existing fleet as its platform matures.

The company has pinned aggressive targets for FY26. It's planning to grow its fleet to 3,000+ units, versus ~1k as of the end of FY25. As of March 25, 2026 (about a week before Pony's earnings release), the company's earnings deck stated that the company had already grown its fleet to 1,400+ units. Deploying ~500 more vehicles per quarter gets Pony to its end-year goal.

Importantly, the company is expecting robotaxi revenue to more than triple y/y versus 2025, implying a minimum of ~$50 million in revenue. On a run rate basis at the end of FY26, using current averages, if 3,000 vehicles generate $50 in daily revenue, across 365 operational days the company can generate $55 million in fare-based revenue alone (which does not include the ancillary licensing revenues included within the robotaxi segment).

Importantly, the company is looking beyond China to grow its robotaxi presence. Of the 20+ cities it aims to expand to in 2026, it is aiming for half to come from new overseas markets. Per CEO Jun Peng's remarks on the Q4 earnings call:

In March, we also entered Hangzhou and Changsha, two thriving top Tier-2 cities. This is just the start, and we will have more cities to follow soon. Now turn to overseas markets. Our presence in Europe, the Middle East, East Asia and Southeast Asia now covers a population of 100 million.

We're aiming for nearly half of our 20 city target to come from overseas by the end of this year. Recently, we teamed up with Uber and Verne, which is a Rimac group company to enter Croatia, working together to launch Europe's first commercial fare charging Robotaxi service.

In the Middle East, we rolled out our first fare charging service with Mowasalat Karwa in Doha, and we are gearing up for fully driverless operations after the approval later this month in Dubai, UAE. In Singapore, we have launched public debut of autonomous driving services with ComfortDelGro. We are confident overseas revenues will grow rapidly in 2026. Ecosystem maturity is a critical pillar in executing our dual engine strategy. Our successful business model makes us a go-to partner.

We find that the company is sufficiently well-capitalized to continue on its early-stage growth trajectory in expanding its robotaxi services. It ended FY25 with $1.62 billion in cash and zero debt (we note this is a substantial one-third chunk of the company's current ~$4.9 billion market cap), compared to -$208.8 million in free cash flow in FY25—suggesting years of liquidity ahead.

We note that especially in international markets, Pony is focusing on joint deployment deals to reduce its capex burden and enable the company to expand more quickly.

Key takeaways

Pony remains an attractive early-stage technology opportunity that is beginning to scale its proven L-4 driving capabilities into more markets, with a push for international penetration in 2026. Stay long here and use this year's dip as a buying opportunity.

data.title: ICAP: High Yield, But Quality Remains Questionable
data.description:
The Infrastructure Capital Equity Income ETF (ICAP) is an ETF managed by Infrastructure Capital Advisors LLC. The fund at present offers a trailing distribution yield of ~9.12% per annum, payable on a monthly basis. Not only does this distribution yield appear to be very strong, but the fund also saw a meaningful capital appreciation over the last year. The meaningful distribution yield combined with the prospect for capital appreciation naturally would appear to be favourable at an initial glance. However, there are significant factors associated both with the quality of distributions of this ETF along with probabilities of price/NAV appreciation.

Performance Evaluation and Investment Methodology

Before moving further onto the specific factors impacting the fund, with reference to performance, the fund at least on a relative basis has not been able to prove its merit. The ETF has underperformed both of its underlying benchmarks; Bloomberg US 500 Dividend Yield Total Return Index and the S&P since inception. The fund saw some outperformance in the current month so far, which might improve the relative comparison for the fund somewhat, but still the broader point of relatively moderate performance is intact.

On an absolute basis however, the ETF so far has done seemingly well. Just over the last one-year period, the fund made a significant ~37% total return, comprising of distributions and capital appreciation. However again, the broader markets have performed similarly over the same period and since the fund primarily allocates to equities, any alpha from underlying security selection is not evident either.

Some may wonder whether this is really a good benchmark to evaluate the performance of an income-focused fund. But this is where it starts getting interesting. Although the fund states the focus to be generating returns by investing in dividend-paying companies, this is how the ETF actually tries to allocate to opportunities. Observe the GARP framework mentioned by the fund. This, on a contextual basis, is not the criteria generally used while investing in ideas with a focus on generating maximum income. Therefore, the comparison with broad-based equities is completely valid to evaluate the performance of this ETF. If the fund is unable to outperform that, I do not see much sense for an investor to allocate.

An investor can rather follow a Systematic Withdrawal Plan over an S&P 500 ETF for that matter as well if there is no desire to distinguish between the dividends and capital gains. Also, the underlying securities of ICAP at present only tend to offer a low to moderate yield post expenses to investors, a point which I will address further in the analysis. The fund also tends to write options and use leverage, largely to support the current high distribution amounts, which again due to their underlying security exposures would probably be fairly difficult to sustain.

Portfolio Allocation

This is how the portfolio presently stands for the fund. The interesting aspect is again there are also names which are not understood to be income-focused equities but rather tend to focus on income growth and capital appreciation in their equities. Just consider the largest allocation Amazon.com, Inc. (AMZN), for example-the company is yet to pay dividends. The other companies do distribute however, but the percentage of distributions is rather moderate on a portfolio level which is reflected by a ~3.5% 30-day sec yield. The high expenses incurred by the ETF also results in SEC yield to stand at a relatively lower range. Nonetheless for investors, this is the real measure of a post-expense yield they stand to make while investing in underlying companies using this ETF.

It would do good to note that earlier it was highlighted the current distribution yield is ~9.12%. So, the differential of roughly ~5.5% should either come from capital appreciation, option writing income, or return of capital. I'll emphasize on this in the next section but again, in my opinion, the current allocation does not seem to align with the objective of the fund.

In an income-focused equity fund, an investor would naturally expect that the underlying allocation would also be suggestive of that objective. In this case however, the fund tries an options overlay along with a small incremental leverage to support the distributions to investors. However, the underlying exposure and its yield on an overall basis doesn’t entirely appear to be suggestive of the said objective of maximizing income.

Quality of Distributions

The ETF distributed significant sums to the shareholders in the last financial year. Moreover, even post distributions, the fund saw an increase in both market price and NAV. The below graph reflects the performance for FY25. This again initially would appear to be a fairly good indicator of the underlying quality, that the NAV even with those payments has risen. But however, the durability of those distributions is what also needs to be assessed carefully.

I looked into the last year’s financial statements and have some interesting aspects to comment upon. Foremost, similar to the 30-day SEC yield, the net investment income of the fund with relative to beginning net asset value comes to even lower ~1.68%. Kindly note this is post expense measure, accounting for fund level expenses including management fees and others and not the gross income. Continuing further, then how is the fund able to distribute a ~9% yield should be the question which should come into the minds of investors.

It would also be good to assess the underlying option writing of the fund. An argument might be made that these call writings might to an extent support the distributions, and that is correct as well. The fund made roughly ~2.5% on average NAV by writing options. However, even if we combine the net investment income and option writing income, still the distribution from earned income would be come to around ~4.5%.

Still however, since options contributed to an extent to the distributable earnings of the fund, it would make sense to reflect a little on this source of funds income. As per the prospectus, ICAP’s adviser may purchase and write both put and call options with these four broader objectives. The options overlay is therefore does not appear to be a pure play, systematic covered-call program which used by many headline-yield ETFs, but rather more of a discretionary tool used alongside security selection.

Importantly, the strategy is only partial in scope: ICAP typically writes calls against roughly 30–40% of its holdings in general, leaving the remaining 60–70% of the portfolio with uncapped upside. This explains why the fund can still participate meaningfully in equity appreciation. The discretionary nature of option strategy with also use cases for a hedge and a vehicle to assist in moving in and out of securities however suggests that this component should also not be looked in isolation with relation to this fund. In addition, the option overlay offers a relatively smaller cushion in drawdowns, meaning options income alone is not sufficient to support a ~9% distribution, which ties back to the earlier point on the heavy reliance on realized capital gains.

Returning to the quality of earnings, the underlying distributions have been largely funded by realized capital gains. It has been the trading of securities of the fund which has allowed it to distribute sums without impairing NAV, at least for the time being. Kindly note that the net realized gain figure of ~$4.27 million is cumulative for both option profits and realized gains from investments. The gains attributed to realized capital gains from investments are around ~$2.62 million, which is ~4%-4.5% of the average assets. The gains attributed to realized capital gains from investments are around ~$2.62 million, which is ~4%-4.5% of the average assets. This is important to reflect upon, since this $2.62 million figure or a ~4.5% of return is of non-recurring nature. And this is what has contributed and largely enabled the fund to distribute payments entirely from distributable earnings instead of return of capital. This directly relates to my earlier comment regarding durability of earnings.

Kindly note that the financial year for ICAP ends in November, and the figures therefore are for the fiscal year ended November 30, 2025. This would not replicate entirely the developments at present; however, they are capable of reflecting the underlying economics behind the fund's distributions and performance.

The point is that the fund would not be expected to continuously realize capital gains and should not be relied upon to do so as well. If the non-recurring gains has been driving the returns, then it's a delicate proposition to be in, especially considering the investment objective of the fund. If it had been set up with an objective of moderate distributions along with capital appreciation, it might still have made sense. However, I don’t consider either the quality or durability of the distributions to be adequate for an income-oriented fund. The investment yield is fairly low, with management expenses further impacting the net investment returns, which is already reflected in the current SEC yield of ~3.5%.

Potential for NAV erosion

The above point takes us into an important point to reflect upon. If we are saying that current high distributions are attributable to capital gains in a very pronounced manner, with over half of the distributable earnings being from selling securities at appreciated value, then will the distribution sustain in case of moderating or falling security prices?

The answer is, in my opinion, for the time being, it might continue to remain healthy, but with a very important caveat, that the distributions would start consuming NAV, also including a component of ‘return of capital’ in addition to distributable earnings. That is what was reflected in the above section that there is a meaningful chance of distributions being funded from beginning-of-period NAV rather than earnings generated during the period. The possibility of this is also dependent on underlying market conditions, which remain outside anyone’s control. To put it in a nutshell, the underlying core income generated by holdings, which is approximately reflected in the SEC yield, is ideally what should be considered a sustainable source, rather than trading-dependent realized/unrealized capital gains, especially when the objective is for income generation.

Higher Expenses, Lower Yield and Related Comparison

The total expenses for ICAP are again fairly high, which causes a meaningful drag on returns. Seeking Alpha mentions them as ~3.19%. However, even if we take the data from Annual Financial Statements, the figure of ~2.47% is significant as well. The portfolio turnover rate is fairly high for ICAP being at ~253% for the last financial year, which also partly explains the meaningful difference between the ~0.8% management fee versus the actual expense ratio for the ETF.

Just to draw parallels, there is one other ETF which is usually compared with ICAP, which is JPMorgan Equity Premium Income ETF (JEPI) . The said fund has an expense ratio of ~0.35%. The magnitude of difference is noteworthy. At the same time however, even after having higher expenses, ICAP has seemingly outperformed JEPI at present. Again, at the same time, however, I always try to caution against past performance as a proxy for future expectations.

The one other aspect which is more meaningful in my opinion versus past performance is again quality of distributions as also reflected by SEC yield. The rationale is simple; I do not want to analyze an income fund with the hope of getting capital appreciation. I already mentioned about Systematic Withdrawal Plan based allocation, which can also be used for investors investing in primarily capital appreciating assets but desiring regular income. With reference to income, JEPI has a SEC yield of ~8.45%. That is almost identical with its trailing distribution yield of ~8.26%. This is the aspect which remains important, that is, distributions being funded by underlying asset incomes, rather than dependence on capital gains realizations.

That said, the two funds have noteworthy differences matter for how the respective yields should be viewed. ICAP directly writes exchange-traded options on around ~30% of its holdings, with modest leverage of 15–25% on the equity book. JEPI while obtains its overlay indirectly through equity-linked notes (ELNs) and typically without leverage. The practical implication is that JEPI’s ~8% yield is at large attributed to ELN premium stream, whereas ICAP’s ~9% continues to depend on realised capital gains that are non-recurring in nature. With reference to the income-specific objective therefore, JEPI appears cleaner for a pure-income mandate, while ICAP’s case rests on a combined distribution-plus-upside proposition rather than on yield quality alone. Some argument could of course be made about the riskiness of underlying securities, and on that basis JEPI might be yielding more with regards to investment income, however that comes secondary. The first test to pass for an income-focused fund is to have adequate income; other aspects come thereafter. The current fund on a relative basis appears to lag JEPI on this count.

On a cumulative basis therefore with current findings, I do not see the durability of earnings which one might want before allocating. The proposition depends largely on contingent factors rather than on the underlying dividends generated by the companies in which assets are allocated. The reason for not considering ICAP to be a sell is the nature of its allocation being in infrastructure, which at least over a near-to-medium term time frame remains attractive.

Infrastructure Outlook Makes It a Hold

ICAP invests in established asset-intensive businesses involved in infrastructure activities, whether utilities, REITs, or energy players. There are significant developments underway across the spectrum which tend to benefit these infrastructure entities. That is also the reason for seeing a meaningful price rise in these companies and ETFs investing in them. The current landscape still appears to be favorable, with demand prospects from data centers, private infrastructure, real estate, housing- residential and commercials and so on, continuing to reflect favorable opportunities down the line.

There is not much doubt that infrastructure is becoming a sector of interest for now. The underlying entities in which ICAP usually allocates capital are asset-heavy, naturally benefitting from infrastructure spending, developments etc. The below graph from McKinsey also suggests the increased interest of LPs in allocating assets to infrastructure as well. However, that in isolation does not warrant an allocation but all that together remains suggestive of favorable sectoral tailwinds for now and also continued investor interest.

This positive outlook regarding the asset-heavy businesses which are necessary across the value chain and sectors is the reason the ETF is kept on a hold at present. The companies of ICAP, with reference to business, would also tend to benefit from these demand surges. Also, even after meaningful price rises, at least dividend-paying infrastructure companies on a relative comparison with broader equities seem to be better comparatively.

The above picture reflects the valuations of top holdings of ICAP at present. For context, the trailing multiple of the S&P 500 presently is ~28, which remains elevated in comparison to these infrastructure players. Not only do these entities have more room to develop, but are priced better than broad-based US equities. This also in a way makes ICAP appear somewhat relatively better than broader equities at present as well. Taking this aspect into account, therefore a hold might be viable for ICAP instead of a suggestion to book profits.

Risk Aspects

The primary aspect is again the quality of the distributions of the fund. The investment proposition is dependent on favorable market conditions to sustain both returns and distributions. The realized returns, either in the form of capital gains on securities or option overlay, warrant certain conditions. The decrease in volatility and premium levels impacts the latter, while corrections would impact the former. The current underlying yield is fairly low to support the current distributions of the ETF. The investment allocation also rests largely contingent on the infrastructure theme playing out. The fund has seen a meaningful rise in recent years and therefore offers a reduced margin of safety. The expense ratios are further very high, and the fund has yet to showcase performance across the cycle. The fund has also seemed to be a beneficiary of the recent run-up in equity returns, also reflecting meaningful correlation with broader equities. In case of sharp market corrections, therefore the fund stands to disappoint investors.

In Conclusions

The distribution quality remains questionable, with payments being attributed to capital gains rather than underlying income. Those capital gains have been largely due to increases in equity prices. The SEC yield presently stands at ~3.5%, which doesn't seem altogether adequate for the fund with an objective to maximize current income. The qualitative business aspects concerning infrastructure developments, however, continue to remain favorable, and therefore a hold rating at present appears to be suitable with regards to this fund.

data.title: Altimmune: Why I'm Downgrading To Hold Despite MASH Promise
data.description:
Investment Overview

Altimmune (ALT) is a Gaithersburg, Maryland based biotech that I have been following for several years, primarily due to its lead drug candidate pemvidutide, a glucagon/GLP-1 dual receptor agonist - not dissimilar in terms of mechanism of action ("MoA") to the wildly successful drug molecules semaglutide and tirzepatide, better known as Wegovy/Ozempic and Zepbound/Mounjaro, in the indications of obesity/Type 2 Diabetes ("T2D"), and marketed and sold by Novo Nordisk (NVO)/Eli Lilly (LLY), earning combined revenues of >$65bn in 2025.

My first take on Altimmune was a negative one - in February 2024, I gave Altimmune stock a Sell rating in a note for Seeking Alpha, which has ultimately proven the right call, as stock has fallen in value by >70% since.

I noted in that post that:

At the end of November 2023 - the same month in which Zepbound was approved - Altimmune shared 48-week data from its Momentum study, this time revealing mean weight loss of 15.6% at the 2.4mg dose, and that >30% of subjects achieved >20% weight loss on the higher 2.4 mg dose at 48 weeks.

in December, analysts at JMP Securities wrote that:

Pemvidutide has a competitive profile based on the top-line MOMENTUM data with greater weight loss than semaglutide, better lipid changes and tolerability than tirzepatide, and better cardiac safety than retatrutide."

Altimmune rose to a value of $12 per share in January 2024, but it has not traded higher since, and the company is no longer even evaluating pemvidutide as an obesity therapy - instead it has pivoted into studies in metabolic dysfunction-associated steatohepatitis (“MASH”), alcohol use disorder ("AUD"), and alcohol associated liver disease ("ALD").

My sell thesis was based around Altimmune's data not being quite strong enough to break the duopoly of semaglutide/tirzepatide, its efficacy seemingly falling slightly below these two molecules, with a higher rate of study discontinuations - ~20% in the MOMENTUM study - suggesting it may not be quite tolerable for the commercial markets either.

In a more recent note shared in December last year, however, I have made Altimmune stock a Buy, given its lower share price and some encouraging data in MASH Phase 2 studies. For example, a lower study discontinuation rate of 9% and the primary endpoint of statistically significant MASH resolution without worsening of fibrosis being met, although the study missed on fibrosis improvement without worsening of MASH.

I suggested that the 48-week MASH data, which would take greater account of non-invasive tests ("NITs") as opposed to biopsy-derived data, was a potentially momentous catalyst for the company and for its future. In this note, I'll comment on that data, which has now been shared, and about my expectations for Altimmune going forward.

Altimmune's 48-Week MASH Data - Positive But Inconclusive

The 48-week data arrived on 19th December - a couple of weeks after my note was published - and unfortunately, stock fell in value from $5 per share to ~$3.5 after it was announced.

Let's take a quick look at the study design as per a presentation shared at the time:

First of all, it's important to note, as per an Altimmune press release, that:

The primary efficacy endpoints, measured at 24 weeks, were MASH resolution without worsening of fibrosis, or fibrosis improvement without worsening of MASH. Secondary endpoints included non-invasive tests of fibrosis and weight loss measured at 24 and 48 weeks.

We know that pemvidutide met one of the study endpoints - MASH resolution and missed one - fibrosis improvement - at 24 weeks, but would the non-invasive test ("NIT") data make a case that pemvidutide can benefit MASH patients in ways that approved drugs or other late-stage pipeline drugs cannot?

Let's share the full data highlights as reported by Altimmune below:

Pemvidutide-treated participants achieved statistically significant reductions in primary non-invasive markers of fibrosis, including Enhanced Liver Fibrosis ("ELF") and Liver Stiffness Measurement (LSM).

  • ELF: 1.2 mg and 1.8 mg doses achieved a mean reduction from baseline of -0.49 and -0.58 respectively, vs. +0.16 in placebo-treated patients (p<0.0001, both doses).
  • LSM: 1.2 mg and 1.8 mg doses achieved a mean reduction from baseline of -3.04 (p<0.05) and -3.97 (p<0.001), respectively, vs. -0.03 in placebo-treated participants.
  • The proportion of participants receiving pemvidutide 1.2 mg and 1.8 mg that achieved both a ≥0.5 reduction in ELF and a 30% reduction in LSM were 27.8% (p<0.001) and 32.4% (p<0.0001) respectively, vs. 3.2% in placebo-treated participants.

Pemvidutide-treated participants also achieved statistically significant reductions in key non-invasive measures of liver health and hepatic inflammation, including liver fat content, alanine aminotransferase (ALT) and corrected T1 (cT1).

  • Liver fat content: 1.2 mg and 1.8 mg doses achieved a mean reduction from baseline of 45.2% and 54.7% respectively, compared to 8.2% in participants who received placebo (p<0.0001).
  • ALT: 1.2 mg and 1.8 mg achieved a mean reduction from baseline of -37.8 IU/L and -37.4 IU/L respectively, vs. -10.3 IU/L in placebo-treated participants (p<0.0001, both doses).
  • cT1: 1.2 mg and 1.8 mg achieved a mean reduction from baseline of -124 and -140 milliseconds ("MS") respectively, vs. -21 ms in placebo-treated participants (p<0.0001, both doses).

Participants receiving pemvidutide 1.2 mg and 1.8 mg achieved weight loss of 4.5% and 7.5%, respectively, vs. 0.2% of placebo-treated participants (p<0.0001, both doses), with no plateauing at 48 weeks with the 1.8 mg dose.

Adverse events leading to treatment discontinuation occurred in 0% and 1.2% of patients treated with pemvidutide 1.2 mg and 1.8 mg, respectively, vs. 3.5% of participants on placebo.

No serious or severe AEs related to treatment were reported.

As I have stated in previous posts, in my view pemvidutide appears to be a "good but not great" drug that seems to have various positive treatment effects - in terms of fibrosis, liver health, weight loss, and MASH resolution - but unlike semaglutide, tirzepatide, and resmetirom - the first-ever drug approved to treat MASH, now marketed and sold by Madrigal Pharmaceuticals as Rezdiffra, earning >$950m of revenues last year - lacks a key competitive advantage.

A cynic might suggest that Eli Lilly and Novo Nordisk simply have more money to spend on clinical studies, while Madrigal may have benefitted from being the earliest to share positive Phase 3 data (Wegovy also secured approval to treat MASH in August 2025). Conversely, a cynic may also argue that Altimmune is trying to bring a drug to market that is not quite in the same class as Rezdiffra, which has a different MoA, being a partial agonist of the thyroid hormone receptor-beta, or Wegovy.

Ultimately, however, Altimmune stock staged a recovery in January this year, as the FDA granted pemvidutide a Breakthrough Therapy Designation ("BTD") for MASH, apparently based on the 24-week IMPACT study data, rising to >$6 per share, and announcing its full-year earnings in early March, the company said it planned to initiate a Phase 3 study in MASH this year. The company also reported:

Cash, cash equivalents and short-term investments of $274 million as of December 31, 2025; Balance sheet further strengthened through $75 million registered direct offering in January 2026

Net loss in 2025 amounted to $(88m), or $(1.00) per share; therefore, it seems likely that Altimmune has secured sufficient funding to complete the 52-week portion of its Phase 3 study in MASH, and file for accelerated approval of the drug, if that study is successful.

As we can see above, Altimmune's quarterly net losses have trended ~$(20m), or greater, and with a Phase 3 study to fund, I would speculate they could grow towards $(30m) per quarter, meaning fresh funding may be required before the beginning of 2028 to ensure the company remains solvent long-term. Such funding will be hard to raise unless Pemvidutide's Phase 3 MASH study is a success.

Looking Ahead - What's The Play On Altimmune Today?

In a recent corporate presentation, Altimmune outlines the design of its Phase 3 study as below:

Probably the first thing to note is that this is an initial 52-week study, and that 52-week data may only support an accelerated approval i.e., approval based on a biomarker indicative of positive treatment effect but not itself a measure of clinical benefit. Any drug that secures accelerated approval must undergo a post-confirmatory study to gain full approval, and if that study fails, the drug can be withdrawn from the market.

Altimmune believes that 60 month data could be sufficient to secure a full approval, and I wonder if that could be used as the post-confirmatory study as opposed to conducting a separate study?

In mid-March, analysts at Truist initiated coverage of Altimmune with a Buy, and a price target of $12, suggesting that the fact that pemvidutide targets glucagon as well as glucagon-like peptide 1 ("GLP-1") will enhance its liver fat-burning capabilities, handing it an edge over Wegovy.

Once again, the investment opportunity here is hard to call. I don't disagree with the analyst, but then again, Eli Lilly's pipeline drug retatrutide targets GLP, glucagon, and glucose-dependent insulinotropic polypeptide ("GIP", which tirzepatide also targets), and an article in Nature summarizes a recent study in patients with obesity as follows:

A 48-week phase 2 obesity study demonstrated weight reductions of 22.8% and 24.2% with retatrutide 8 and 12 mg, respectively. The primary objective of this substudy was to assess mean relative change from baseline in liver fat (LF) at 24 weeks in participants from that study with metabolic dysfunction-associated steatotic liver disease and ≥10% of LF.

Here, in this randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled trial, participants (n = 98) were randomly assigned to 48 weeks of once-weekly subcutaneous retatrutide (1, 4, 8 or 12 mg dose) or placebo. The mean relative change from baseline in LF at 24 weeks was −42.9% (1 mg), −57.0% (4 mg), −81.4% (8 mg), −82.4% (12 mg) and +0.3% (placebo) (all P < 0.001 versus placebo).

At the highest dose, retatrutide appears to show superiority to pemvidutide, which makes me wonder if Altimmune can genuinely compete in a MASH market that may become increasingly crowded in the coming years.

Consider the company's own analysis of its competition, in its 2025 annual report/10K filing, as quoted below:

We face competition for pemvidutide, our glucagon/GLP-1 dual agonist for the treatment of MASH from companies such as Madrigal Pharmaceuticals (MDGL), Aligos ((ALGS)) and Viking Therapeutics ((VKTX)), which are developing orally administered, thyroid hormone receptor (“THR”) β-selective agonists; Novo Nordisk ((NVO)), Roche ((RHHBY)), and GSK (GSK), which are developing fibroblast growth factor 21 (“FGF-21”) analogs; Novo Nordisk, which is developing a GLP-1 agonist; Merck (MRK)/Hanmi Pharmaceutical and Boehringer Ingelheim, which are developing GLP-1/glucagon dual agonists; Eli Lilly (LLY), which is developing a glucose-dependent insulinotropic polypeptide receptor (“GIP”)/GLP-1 dual agonist and a GLP-1 GIP/glucagon triple agonist; Roche which is developing a GLP-1/GIP dual agonist; AstraZeneca (AZN) developing a GLP-1/glucagon and amylin combination; Inventiva (IVA), which is developing a pan-peroxisome proliferator-activated receptor (“PPAR”) agonist; Sagimet (SGMT) which is developing a fatty acid synthetase inhibitor, Apollo Therapeutics Group Limited/HEC Pharma which is developing a GLP-1/FGF-21 dual agonist; and Pfizer (PFE), Roche, Gilead (GILD), AstraZeneca (AZN), and Eli Lilly (LLY), which are developing small molecule GLP-1 agonists. In addition, many other small companies are developing other new technologies directed towards MASH.

It is a formidable list of competitors, encompassing a wide range of MoAs, GLP-1 drug candidates acquired from China based pharmas, developed in house, acquired for billions of dollars (in the case of the FGF21 class), being developed by companies with almost limitless funding compared to Altimmune.

In many ways, I feel sorry for Altimmune because the company has guided its drug through some broadly successful studies in obesity and in MASH, and I wonder, in terms of some of the M&A deals and partnerships that have taken place in recent years in the MASH/GLP-1 space, if acquiring Altimmune and Pemvidutide might have been a better option for some companies discussed above, given its progress to date (Merck and AstraZeneca, for example, acquired preclinical GLP-1 assets).

It's possible that data from Altimmune's study in AUD, which will read out later this year, could trigger some excitement on Wall Street, perhaps enable Altimmune to raise funding at a decent share price and valuation, and fund the company all the way through a successful phase and regulatory filing, at which point the company could raise further funding at an even higher price and target $500m-$1bn in annual revenues.

That, in my view, is the bull case for Altimmune, but personally, given the intense competition, the "good, but not great" profile of pemvidutide, the lack of funding in relation to rivals, the pivoting from one disease indication to another, the long wait for MASH data, and the original missed fibrosis endpoint in the Phase 2 study, I have to downgrade my rating on Altimmune stock from a "Buy" to a "Hold".

I don't rule out the possibility that a Pharma takes a punt on Altimmune and pemvidutide and pursues an M&A deal - I would absolutely not have predicted that 89Bio would be acquired for ~$3.5bn by Pharma giant Roche (79% premium to traded price at the time), for example - but the risk that pemvidutide's race could be run if the Phase 3 MASH data is not stellar means I am moving to the sidelines and downgrading my Buy Rating To A Hold.

There is evidence to show that Altimmune's NIT data shows slight superiority to the likes of tirzepatide, semaglutide, resmetirom, and Akero Therapeutics efruxifermin (Akero has itself now been acquired by Novo Nordisk for ~$4.7bn), but to take on such a wide range of candidates and battle successfully for market share in a few years' time feels like a difficult challenge - by no means impossible, but perhaps unlikely.

data.description:

Composite returns are preliminary. Past performance is not indicative of future results.

2026 started out well, with global growth tracking slightly above expectations. The U.S. Federal Reserve held its benchmark rate at 3.5%–3.75% following a December cut, and several other G20 central banks maintained or reduced rates, creating a broadly supportive macro backdrop through January and February. Cyclicals drove strong early returns, even as U.S. tariff threats continued. March brought a decisive turn. The war in Iran and the closure of the Strait of Hormuz sent energy prices sharply higher. Both the European Central Bank and Bank of Japan held rates steady but cautioned that the ongoing conflict poses risks of rising inflation and slowing economic growth. The pivot reshuffled sector leadership — lifting energy and defensive positions while tamping down AI-adjacent cyclicals.

On this backdrop, the Polaris International Equity Composite gained 5.13% (net of fees), outperforming the MSCI EAFE Index, which returned -1.12%. The Composite outperformed in most sectors including double-digit gains from materials, energy and utilities, while information technology and health care added measurably. Absolute detractors included consumer discretionary, industrials and communication services. Holdings in Norway, Italy, Japan Spain and the Netherlands contributed most, along with off-benchmark countries including Canada and South Korea. China declined on its own domestic challenges (constrained manufacturing, lower consumption), while a handful of French and U.K. stocks in unrelated industries tempered results.

First Quarter 2026 Performance Analysis

Materials were among the top contributors to performance during the quarter. The closure of the Strait of Hormuz had ripple effects across global commodity markets, disrupting roughly 30% of the world's nitrogen supply — a key ingredient in fertilizer — sending prices higher. Norwegian fertilizer producer Yara International (YARIY) was largely insulated from the disruption, and picked up market share from competitors who couldn't deliver. Methanex Corp. (MEOH) was another strong contributor in the materials sector, benefiting from supply disruptions on two fronts — first from natural gas shortages, then from the Hormuz closure — both of which pushed methanol prices higher. Crucially, the company had more product to sell at those elevated prices, having added over 20% to its global production capacity through the 2025 acquisition of OCI Global's methanol business. More volume at higher prices proved to be a powerful combination, and we sold the position at a healthy profit having reached our valuation target. Lundin Mining (LUNMF) capitalized on tight copper supply and strong performance at its Caserones mine, while the Vicuna joint venture bolstered the long-term growth outlook.

Energy stocks also contributed to performance in absolute terms. Eni S.p.A. (E) and TotalEnergies SE (TTE) advanced strongly as the market repriced energy businesses in response to the Strait of Hormuz closure and surging oil prices. Both Eni and TotalEnergies entered the period having reported robust 2025 annual earnings, driven by strong operational performance and production growth. Both companies have focused on strong cash generation and ongoing shareholder returns, reinforcing investor willingness to hold in a heated commodity market.

Stocks within the IT sector had a strong start to the year, driven by the AI boom creating genuine shortages in advanced computer chips and memory. SK hynix Inc. controls 57%+ of the high bandwidth memory (HBM) market, and its entire 2026 HBM production is already sold out. Samsung Electronics (SSNLF) confirmed its next-generation HBM4 chips are on track for delivery to NVIDIA (NVDA) in early 2026. We exited Capgemini SE (CAPMF), a French tech consulting firm, after growing concern that its corporate clients would handle more IT work inhouse rather than outsource it.

The Composite's financial holdings outpaced the benchmark's sector returns. DNB Bank (DNBBY) led the way with double-digit gains, buoyed by strong earnings, a robust Norwegian economy, and upward analyst revisions — with Popular Inc. (BPOP) and Shinhan Financial Group (SHG) also contributing positively. Offsetting these results were meaningful declines from Ping An Insurance Group (PNGAY), which bore the weight of China macro pessimism, and further pressure from German reinsurers.

Industrials delivered mixed performance, with gains from top 10 contributor Marubeni Corp. (MARUY) offset by weakness elsewhere in the sector. Japanese trading house Marubeni reported an upward revision to its full-year profit forecast on a bullish stance for copper prices, guiding for increasing annual dividends and committing to an additional share buyback. International Consolidated Airlines Group (ICAGY) faced pressure in March as oil's sharp move and the "risk-off" tone hit economically-sensitive transport names simultaneously. Teleperformance (TLPFF) faced persistent investor fears that generative AI will structurally disrupt the customer service outsourcing industry.

Same theme, different industry: French communication services company Publicis Groupe (PUBGY) entered 2026 ranked #1 in 2025 global new-business performance; however, the company declined as it could not shake investor fears that generative AI will structurally erode the profitability of traditional advertising agency models.

Within consumer discretionary stocks, Kia Corp. was a consistent bright spot, as solid U.S. sales, aggressive growth guidance, and enthusiasm around its hybrid EV expansion, robotics, and AI positioning helped the stock look past near-term tariff concerns. Record 2025 annual sales in both the U.S. and India, led by SUV and hybrid demand, bolstered the outlook. Offsetting this, Alibaba Group (BABA) struggled as investors questioned the return on investment from its $52 billion AI and cloud infrastructure commitment through 2027. Sony Group (SONY) also declined, pressured by memory chip price spikes that threatened PlayStation 5 margins and the delayed release of several popular software titles.

Over the course of the quarter, we sold Methanex Corp. at a profit as it met our target valuation level, while Capgemini was exited as evolving macro conditions eroded our original thesis. On the buy side, we initiated a position in Ryanair Holdings (RYAAY), Europe's largest budget airline and a name we previously owned during the pandemic. The oil-driven selloff pushed the stock to a price we found attractive, and while higher fuel costs are a headwind for all airlines, Ryanair's structural cost advantages and 80% hedged fuel costs give it more room to absorb the pressure than U.S. full-service carriers. With European air travel supply still tight relative to demand, we see Ryanair as well positioned to keep taking customers from weaker competitors.

Investment Environment and Strategy

The Middle East conflict and the Strait of Hormuz closure have introduced a level of volatility that is unlikely to resolve quickly. Oil prices at current levels are not sustainable for a global economy that is already soft in many regions, and the prospect of rate cuts — which markets were counting on — has effectively been taken off the table. That said, not everything about this environment works against us. Higher rates, while a drag on growth, tend to benefit our financial holdings and discourage speculative investing that has contributed to growth dominance over the past few years. We will continue to look selectively for opportunities where volatility creates dislocations, adding quality names at attractive valuations.

The more fundamental shift — and the one we believe has the most lasting significance — is the growing recognition that owning only U.S. stocks is no longer a winning strategy. For the better part of 18 months, geopolitical stress, dollar uncertainty, and uneven global growth have been quietly building the case for international diversification. That case is now impossible to ignore. Investors with a heavy U.S. bias are increasingly wary; the instinct to look beyond American borders is accelerating. This is precisely where we have been positioned, and we believe an international portfolio is as well-suited to the current environment as any we have seen in years.

Performance from the inception date through March 31, 1995 represents the portfolio track record established by Portfolio Manager Bernard Horn while affiliated with a prior firm. It should not be considered as a recommendation to purchase or sell a particular security mentioned, may change at any time and may not represent current or future investments. data.title: Hercules Capital: Big Commitments, Big Premium And Big Software Risk
data.description:
Elevator pitch

Here are the puts and takes I see for the business development company [BDC] Hercules Capital, Inc. (HTGC):

  1. Portfolio growth indicators are strong
  2. Increased chances of a rate cut is a headwind but HTGC has reduced the risk somewhat
  3. HTGC has significant exposure to the software sector, which is prone to AI disruption
  4. HTGC is trading at trough-level discounts vs its BDC peers but is also at a premium to NAV
  5. HTGC is stuck in a big monthly range

Portfolio Growth Indicators are Strong

HTGC has been securing a greater amount of investment commitments based on the latest data:

Management's commentary is upbeat with a rosy deal environment outlook:

our momentum accelerated in Q1 — marked by an all-time high in new debt and equity commitments... We were able to expand commitments to several portfolio companies during Q1 in addition to closing new commitments with 16 new portfolio companies during the first quarter... As we look ahead, we believe we are well positioned to benefit from the current market conditions which are creating a more favorable originations environment in 2026.

- CEO Scott Bluestein in HTGC Press Release

I expect this lead to continued growth in total portfolio value:

And this can increase investment income and net interest margin dollars:

Increased chances of a rate cut is a headwind but HTGC has reduced the risk somewhat

Looking forward to the start of next year in Jan'27, the chances of the Fed Funds rate being lower than the current 350-375bps has increased over the past couple of months:

This is a headwind for HTGC as lower rates weigh down on net income. But the good news is HTGC has made its portfolio less sensitive to interest rate changes to mitigate this risk:

HTGC has significant exposure to the software sector, which is prone to AI disruption

In my recent article on the Ares Capital (ARCC), I discussed at length why I believe high software exposure portfolios are a risk in business development companies (BDCs). The one line explanation is that AI agents can lower the cost of software development, affecting traditional software's pricing both directly and indirectly via reduced seats as enterprises realize efficiencies with leaner workforces. I suggest reading this article here for more context.

HTGC has around 35% of its portfolio in application and system software based on the latest quarter's investor presentation:

This kind of exposure is comparable to Ares Capital's 38% weightage toward the Software Services industry. So I view this as a hidden risk as headwinds to these companies can impact HTGC's financials via NAV erosion and worse credit quality. To that end, it is useful to track overall credit rating metrics:

Note that a lower number represents a higher quality portfolio.

I will note that management is cognizant of the high software exposure risk and has committed to being more selective in software deals by seeking companies with a hardware moat or presence in highly regulated markets that are less prone to AI disruption due to data accessibility constraints:

Hercules factors this into our technology underwritings, and our focus over the last 12 to 18 months has largely been centered on software companies with a hardware moat or with customer bases that are highly regulated.

- CEO Scott Bluestein in the Q4 FY25 earnings call

HTGC is trading at trough-level discounts vs its BDC peers but is also at a premium to NAV

HTGC's 1-yr fwd PE right now is 8.10x. This is 4.4% lower than the BDC sectoral median multiple of 8.47x:

I have taken the companies in the VanEck BDC Income ETF (BIZD) to form the peer set in the analysis above.

Over the longer term, HTGC has on average traded at a 5.9% to the broader BDC sector. So a 4.4% discount right now makes its valuation look relatively attractive. Also note how HTGC's discount vs the sectoral median is near the trough levels in the chart above. I believe this increases the chances of us seeing the valuation gap vs peer contract.

Looking at valuations through the P/NAV, HTGC may look a bit expensive as it is trading at 29% higher than NAV:

Given decent exposure to the software sector in HTGC's portfolio, I worry if HTGC's premium to NAV is really justified.

HTGC is stuck in a big monthly range

If this is your first time reading a Hunting Alpha article using Technical Analysis, you may want to read this post, which explains how and why I read the charts the way I do. All my charts reflect total shareholder return as they are adjusted for dividends/distributions.

HTGC Technical Analysis

HTGC finds itself in a big monthly range. A strong bearish push from resistance has been unable to break the support lows as the bulls have bounced from the key level. I expect prices to remain largely rangebound.

Takeaway and Positioning

  • Fundamental Thesis: Neutral as portfolio growth tailwinds are offset by high software sector exposure and net income headwinds in a likely rate cut scenario
  • Valuation: Neutral as the trough-level discounts vs BDC peers on a 1-yr fwd PE basis is offset by a large premium to NAV
  • Technical Analysis: Neutral as HTGC stock is stuck in a monthly range
  • Final Assessment and Rating: Neutral, Hold

data.title: Danaher Q1 2026 Earnings Update
data.description:
Danaher (DHR) had another somewhat uneventful quarter.

Looking at Danaher’s numbers every quarter is a stark reminder of just how anemic the growth has proved to be for a company that enjoys some supposedly secular tailwinds. Even if you adjust for their spin-off of Environmental & Applied Solutions segment in 2023, their topline in 1Q’23 would be $5.95 Billion. Three years later in 1Q’26, their topline remains the same: $5.95 Billion!

The picture is similar in their biotechnology segment. The inventory destocking that affected them in 2023-24 period is clearly behind them, but growth is still uninspiring at ~6-7%. Sartorius Stedim Biotech (SDMHF), which is more of a pure-play bioprocessing comp, will announce their earnings tomorrow, but their trend has been somewhat similar over the last few years.

But there are signs that we may see a better growth trajectory in the second half and beyond. I will particularly highlight management’s commentary on order growth:

Equipment declined modestly in Q1, but we were encouraged to see orders growth of more than 30%, marking the first quarter of year-over-year equipment order growth in nearly 2 years.

QoQ decline is not surprising since there is some seasonality to it, but if order growth continues at a sustained pace, Danaher’s bioprocessing business should return to comfortable double digit growth.

Margin in biotechnology segment improved YoY to reach 42.7% in 1Q’26. For context, margin in this segment peaked at 46.4% in 2021.

Life Sciences segment’s core revenue grew at only 0.5% despite having an easy comp of -4% in 1Q’25. At least, that’s better than the Diagnostics segment whose core revenue declined by 4% in 1Q’26 despite, again, pretty easy comp of -1.5% in 1Q’25. I won’t bore you with details of headwinds described by management, but these segments have been going through some really uninspiring performance for a while now.

The more inspiring bit during the call yesterday was when an analyst asked about AI’s potential impact on Danaher’s Life Sciences and Diagnostics segment. Rainer Blair, CEO of Danaher, said the following in response (emphasis mine):

“…we think AI is going to be a growth accelerator for the pharma and biotech industry, both in the near and in the long term. And the reason for that is we think that AI will accelerate the drug development and commercialization flywheel and result in better development pipeline yields. So as you know, the average yield in the drug development pipeline today is just above 10%. There’s an enormous opportunity here to improve the yield of the pipeline and to accelerate the biopharma flywheel along with the flywheels of life science tool providers like ourselves.

And so this improved yield drives both growth and profitability and reinvestment in the pharma industry. And that, of course, in turn, drives more investment into discovery, including wet lab validation, development in the clinic as well as commercial drug manufacturing. So in the short term, what we’re seeing actually is incremental more demand, which we expect to accelerate in the building of biologic models. Autonomous science is the current buzzword that refers to the building of biologic models, and of course, that requires automation, which we’re very well represented in. It requires more analytical instruments and it requires more reagents as well. So that’s the short-term impact as this practically new market segment of autonomous science starts to play out here, and that plays out first in discovery and then continues to accelerate through the development pipeline.

And of course, we’re very well positioned here with our life science tools. I mentioned automation, analytical instruments that, of course, increasingly are AI-enabled reagents that support all of those models going forward. And that’s a several year driver. These biologic models are in the single-digit percentage of information coverage required, very different than large language models. These biologic models require significantly more information in order to become general use type of model. So that’s the short term.

And as I indicated then in the long term, what we’re going to see is the cycle time of pharma development being compressed and the hit rate, i.e., the yield to be increased. And that flywheel is going to be very good for patients. It’s going to be very good for the pharma industry and those partners like ourselves that support that industry.

Now as you think about that going through development… these more commercialized drugs mean more business for our bioprocessing business. We're the best positioned there with the broadest and deepest portfolio… then lastly, a lot of these drugs are going to be more sophisticated. They are going to require more sophisticated, more accurate diagnostics. If they're not personalized diagnostics, they will require near personalized diagnostics to come online.”

I haven’t done enough work to assess Danaher management’s point of view, but let me share what gives me a bit of a pause here. Last week, Max Jaderberg, Chief AI Officer, and Sergei Yakneen, Chief Technology Officer at Isomorphic Labs appeared on Nvidia AI (NVDA) podcast and shared a vision for the future that may have a bit of tension with the future Danaher likes to imagine. Notice what Nvidia mentioned in their blog (emphasis mine):

“By modeling cellular processes with AI, Isomorphic’s teams can predict molecular interactions with exceptional accuracy. Their advanced AI models enable scientists to computationally simulate how potential therapeutics interact with their targets in complex biological systems. Using AI to reduce dependence on wet lab experiments accelerates the drug discovery pipeline and creates possibilities for addressing previously untreatable conditions.”

If AI lets you do an order of magnitude more simulation than you ever could through actual experimentation, wouldn’t that be a potentially secular headwind for at least some part of Danaher’s business? As I have mentioned before, I haven’t done enough work here, so I don’t have a strong opinion yet. But I’m also quite not ready to believe the halcyon future of AI being a secular tailwind for Danaher that management likes me to believe.

Management kept their core revenue guidance of ~3-6% but raised the high end of 2026 EPS guidance from $8.5 to $8.55.

On capital allocation, Danaher maintains its bias to do more M&A. They have already announced they’re going to acquire Masimo (MASI) for $9.9 Billion. Management mentioned that they believe the acquisition will be accretive at all levels (gross and operating margins) and reach HSD ROIC in year 5. HSD ROIC in year 5? This type of uninspiring ROIC is exactly why I decided to sell the stock late last year. Scuttleblurb in a more recent post also explained why such ROIC trajectory is unlikely to change anytime soon, if ever (emphasis mine):

“The high-single-digit ROIC that management expects to achieve on Masimo within 5 years may be disappointing to those who remember the days when the bogey for large platform acquisitions was set at 10%. But targets of significant enough size to move the needle for a buyer as large as Danaher are in scarce supply and generally aren’t managed by idiots who sell at cyclical bottoms. Everyone knows these are valuable properties, and I expect them to be taken out at prices that reflect this reality. The days of opportunistically seizing hidden gems at single-digit forward EBITA multiples are long over.

I don’t doubt that Danaher will pull out of the current growth slump and eventually return to high-single-digit organic growth. China is recovering, A&G demand will eventually come back, and the biologics tailwind remains as strong as ever. Over time, AI may accelerate the pace of drug discovery and streamline processes downstream of that. But it’s reasonable to expect that most of whatever free cash flow drops down from that will be deployed into M&A, the returns from which will drive much of the returns that shareholders realize. Over the last decade, Danaher has spent ~$47bn on life sciences-related acquisitions. EBITDA (ex. spin-offs) has grown by $5.6bn over the same period, implying somewhere in the neighborhood of ~9% after-tax returns over that period (if anything, this is generous since my calculation implicitly gives acquisitions credit for all of the $5.6bn increase in EBITDA over the past 10 years). Danaher will continue to compound value for shareholders, though perhaps at a more modest pace over the next 10 years compared to the last 20.”

Indeed, the next 10 years will likely be more modest than the last 20 for the stock. The starting valuation, despite the stock being flat for the last five years is still on the high-teens. You can, of course, rightly argue that the quality of the business Danaher owns today is likely much better than the collection of industrial assets it owned in the 2000-10 period, but the point still remains.

Market Review

Equity performance was broadly negative across developed and emerging markets. Heightened geopolitical tensions involving Iran and the subsequent spike in oil prices weighed on risk sentiment despite resilient, albeit slowing, global growth and moderating inflation. The S&P 500® Index declined 4.33% for the quarter. Within the index, six of the 11 Global Industry Classification Standard (GICS®) equity sectors finished in positive territory, led by energy and materials. Value stocks outperformed growth stocks. Among other major equity benchmarks, the MSCI EAFE Index, a measure of developed markets excluding the U.S. and Canada, decreased by 1.24%, while the MSCI Emerging Markets Index decreased by 0.17%.

In our view, headline risk will remain elevated for the foreseeable future and will lead to more dramatic risk on/risk off days for the market.

Quarterly Performance

BNY Mellon Dynamic Value Fund (Class A at NAV)(DAGVX) returned 2.23% during the first quarter of 2026. In comparison, the fund's benchmark, the Russell 1000 Value Index (the "Index"), returned 2.10% for the same time period.

The Net Expenses is the actual fund expense ratio applicable to investors. Not all classes of shares may be available to all investors or through all broker-dealer platforms.

Market Review (continued)

In the U.S., equity markets delivered negative returns during the quarter, with the Dow Jones Industrial Average® declining 3.19% and the NASDAQ 100 Index® falling 5.82%. U.S. fixed-income markets were broadly flat over the period. Geopolitical developments in the Middle East, including the effective closure of the Strait of Hormuz, contributed to elevated market volatility and increased downside risks later in the quarter. Against this backdrop, the U.S. economy remained relatively resilient. The Federal Reserve held rates steady as inflation eased from 2.7% year over year in December to 2.2% in February. Labor markets remained solid, with payroll growth rebounding following a weaker December reading and unemployment edging down to 4.2%. Consumer data were mixed: sentiment fell to a multiyear low in January before improving through March, while retail spending softened and then stabilized. Meanwhile, manufacturing remained in contraction, highlighting persistent weakness in the industrial sector. The U.S. dollar declined against a basket of major currencies.

Equity markets in developed economies were broadly lower, with the UK and Japan among the primary exceptions. The eurozone continued to expand gradually, with gross domestic product (GDP) growth of 1.4% (annualized) in the fourth quarter of 2025, slightly above expectations. Purchasing Managers' Indices (PMIs) edged higher through March, while inflation eased from 1.7% in January to 1.6% in February. The European Central Bank (ECB) held rates steady, signaling patience amid ongoing structural and geopolitical headwinds. In the UK, inflation also trended lower, falling from 3.4% in December to 2.8% in February, although the Bank of England kept rates unchanged at 3.75% amid continued uncertainty. Japan's economy remained stable, with inflation holding around 1.5% and the Bank of Japan maintaining an accommodative stance while monitoring wage dynamics. Initial fourth-quarter 2025 GDP growth was weak at approximately 0.2% (annualized) and below expectations, weighing on sentiment and the yen, before being revised higher to 1.3% (annualized) later in the quarter.

Emerging-market equities posted negative returns for the quarter, although they outperformed their developed-market peers. In China, full-year growth met the government's 5% target for 2025, though activity slowed to 4.5% year over year in the fourth quarter. At the start of the year, PMIs slipped back into contraction in January, and inflation eased from 0.8% to 0.2%, before activity showed modest improvement in March, with manufacturing returning to expansion. India remained a relative bright spot, with PMI data indicating solid, though slightly moderating, expansion amid rising input costs. In Latin America, Brazil's central bank initially held rates steady as inflation hovered near 4.5%, but began easing in March, lowering the policy rate to 14.75% as price pressures leveled off. Mexico's central bank kept rates on hold at 7.00% amid persistent inflation concerns and a delayed path back to target. Geopolitical developments and changes in U.S. policy toward Venezuela, including adjustments to oil export licenses, added uncertainty during the quarter but supported a modest recovery in Venezuelan crude output.

Commodities moved higher during the period, with the Refinitiv/CoreCommodity CRB Index rising 25.78%. Commodity markets were influenced by shifting supply dynamics and heightened geopolitical risk. Oil prices trended upward throughout the quarter—ending January and February near multi-month highs and rising sharply in March—supported by supply discipline from the Organization of the Petroleum Exporting Countries and its allies (OPEC+), resilient demand, and increased geopolitical tensions involving Iran and the Strait of Hormuz. Natural-gas prices rose early in the quarter amid cold-weather demand before reversing in February and March as ample U.S. supply weighed on the market. Precious metals generally advanced early in the quarter, supported by safe-haven demand and a weaker U.S. dollar, before pulling back in March, while copper declined in February amid concerns about softer demand.

Performance Review

The fund (Class A shares at NAV) outperformed its benchmark, the Russell 1000 Value Index, during the first quarter of 2026. An overweight to the energy sector added the most to relative returns; stock selection in the industrials sector was also beneficial. Conversely, an underweight and stock selection in the consumer staples and utilities sectors detracted.

Sector Review1

Positive Impacts

Energy: An overweight in the sector was the leading contributor to relative returns during the quarter, supported by soaring crude prices due to the war in Iran. Leading contributors included Marathon Petroleum Corporation, Permian Resources Corporation Class A, and Exxon Mobil Corporation.

Industrials: Stock selection in the industrials sector also contributed, most notably among aerospace and defense holdings.

Negative Impacts

Consumer Staples: Stock selection and an underweight to consumer staples sector detracted as market volatility increased demand for defensive sectors. Additionally, the position in Estee Lauder Companies Class A was a leading detractor.

Utilities: An underweight and stock selection in the utilities sector detracted.

Stock Review1

Positive Impacts

Applied Materials (AMAT): Applied Materials manufactures semiconductor and display equipment and is viewed as a major beneficiary of artificial intelligence (AI) data center expansion. In its earnings report, the firm forecasted better-than-expected quarterly results, helped by increased chipmaking tool orders driven by AI demand.

Marathon Petroleum Corporation (MPC): This leading independent downstream and midstream energy company gained due to the sharp rally in crude prices, sparked by the war in Iran and threats to energy flows coming through the Strait of Hormuz.

Permian Resources Corporation Class A (PR): This leading independent oil and natural gas company gained due to the sharp rally in energy prices.
